abstract: Memory has emerged, and will continue to remain, a core capability of foundation model-based agents. As research on agent memory rapidly expands and attracts unprecedented attention, the field has also become increasingly fragmented. Existing works that fall under the umbrella of agent memory often differ substantially in their motivations, implementations, and evaluation protocols, while the proliferation of loosely defined memory terminologies has further obscured conceptual clarity. Traditional taxonomies such as long/short-term memory have proven insufficient to capture the diversity of contemporary agent memory systems. This work aims to provide an up-to-date landscape of current agent memory research. We begin by clearly delineating the scope of agent memory and distinguishing it from related concepts such as LLM memory, retrieval augmented generation (RAG), and context engineering. We then examine agent memory through the unified lenses of forms, functions, and dynamics. From the perspective of forms, we identify three dominant realizations of agent memory, namely token-level, parametric, and latent memory. From the perspective of functions, we propose a finer-grained taxonomy that distinguishes factual, experiential, and working memory. From the perspective of dynamics, we analyze how memory is formed, evolved, and retrieved over time. To support practical development, we compile a comprehensive summary of memory benchmarks and open-source frameworks. Beyond consolidation, we articulate a forward-looking perspective on emerging research frontiers, including memory automation, reinforcement learning integration, multimodal memory, multi-agent memory, and trustworthiness issues. We hope this survey serves not only as a reference for existing work, but also as a conceptual foundation for rethinking memory as a first-class primitive in the design of future agentic intelligence.

keyInsights: Agent memory differs from LLM memory, RAG, and context engineering by focusing on an external, evolving cognitive state for persistent, adaptive decision-making.

Memory forms are classified by *where it resides* and *how it's represented* (token-level, parametric, latent), with token-level memory further structured into flat, planar, and hierarchical organizations.
Memory functions are categorized by *purpose* (factual, experiential, working memory), while dynamics cover the lifecycle of memory via *formation*, *evolution*, and *retrieval* processes.
originalProblem: The rapid expansion of AI agent research has led to fragmentation and conceptual ambiguity regarding memory systems.
Existing taxonomies, like the simple long-term/short-term dichotomy, are insufficient to capture the diversity of modern agent memory.
A lack of clear distinctions between agent memory, LLM memory, Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), and context engineering hinders systematic progress.
results: The survey identifies token-level memory as the most prevalent form, detailing its structural variations (1D flat, 2D planar, 3D hierarchical) alongside parametric and latent memory forms.

It presents a functional taxonomy distinguishing factual memory (user/environment consistency), experiential memory (case-based, strategy-based, skill-based learning), and working memory (single/multi-turn context management).

Memory dynamics are decomposed into formation (summarization, distillation, structured construction), evolution (consolidation, updating, forgetting), and retrieval (timing, query construction, strategies, post-processing), each with detailed sub-processes.
solution: The authors introduce a multidimensional taxonomy for agent memory based on "Forms, Functions, and Dynamics" (FFD).
They rigorously define agent memory and delineate its boundaries from related concepts such as LLM memory, RAG, and context engineering.
The survey systematically categorizes existing research within this FFD framework and outlines emerging research frontiers.
paper_summary.summary: This survey provides a comprehensive analysis of memory systems in AI agents, introducing a novel "Forms–Functions–Dynamics" taxonomy to categorize and understand current research. It clarifies conceptual boundaries with related areas and identifies promising future research directions, establishing a unified framework for the field.

title: Memory in the Age of AI Agents

abstract: Recent Large Reasoning Models (LRMs), such as DeepSeek-R1 and OpenAI o1, have demonstrated strong performance gains by scaling up the length of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning during inference. However, a growing concern lies in their tendency to produce excessively long reasoning traces, which are often filled with redundant content (e.g., repeated definitions), over-analysis of simple problems, and superficial exploration of multiple reasoning paths for harder tasks. This inefficiency introduces significant challenges for training, inference, and real-world deployment (e.g., in agent-based systems), where token economy is critical. In this survey, we provide a comprehensive overview of recent efforts aimed at improving reasoning efficiency in LRMs, with a particular focus on the unique challenges that arise in this new paradigm. We identify common patterns of inefficiency, examine methods proposed across the LRM lifecycle, i.e., from pretraining to inference, and discuss promising future directions for research. To support ongoing development, we also maintain a real-time GitHub repository tracking recent progress in the field. We hope this survey serves as a foundation for further exploration and inspires innovation in this rapidly evolving area.

author_info.institution: Hong Kong University of Science and Technology

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
Huazhong University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: Reasoning efficiency, defined as the ratio of solution quality to computational cost, is crucial for maximizing "intelligence per token" in LRMs.
Inefficiency in LRMs manifests as redundant content, overthinking simple questions, and incoherent or suboptimal reasoning paths, requiring targeted interventions.

Achieving efficient reasoning necessitates addressing challenges such as quantifying the utility of individual reasoning steps, controlling thinking length dynamically, and overcoming architectural bottlenecks of Transformers.
originalProblem: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) generate excessively long, often redundant, and inefficient reasoning traces (Chain-of-Thought), leading to high computational costs for training and inference.
Existing surveys on LRMs have not specifically addressed the growing challenge of reasoning efficiency, leaving a gap in synthesized knowledge for this critical area.
The inherent verbosity of LRMs creates a "token economy" problem, impacting memory consumption, inference latency, and overall computational expenses, thus hindering real-world applicability.
results: Efficient inference techniques include length budgeting, dynamic switching between models or systems, and parallel search strategies to prune suboptimal reasoning paths.
Supervised fine-tuning methods enable reasoning chain compression through data engineering or facilitate latent-space reasoning where explicit token generation is minimized.
Reinforcement learning approaches utilize explicit length penalties or implicit biases from preference modeling to guide models toward generating optimal and concise reasoning chains.

Pre-training strategies focus on inherent efficiency through latent space reasoning or by adopting subquadratic attention mechanisms and linear recurrent structures to overcome Transformer limitations.
solution: The survey formally defines reasoning efficiency and identifies common patterns of LRM inefficiency (e.g., redundant content, overthinking) along with unique challenges.

It systematically categorizes and details methods for improving reasoning efficiency across the entire LRM lifecycle: during inference, with supervised fine-tuning (SFT), through reinforcement learning (RL), and during pre-training.
The authors provide a comprehensive taxonomy of techniques within each stage and maintain a real-time GitHub repository to serve as a dynamic community resource.
paper_summary.summary: A collaborative survey by researchers from Shanghai AI Laboratory and other institutions maps current techniques for enhancing reasoning efficiency in Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) across their lifecycle. It systematically categorizes approaches to mitigate verbose and computationally expensive reasoning traces, aiming to optimize "intelligence per token" for practical LRM deployment.

title: A Survey of Efficient Reasoning for Large Reasoning Models: Language, Multimodality, and Beyond

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have delivered impressive results in language understanding, generation, reasoning, and pushes the ability boundary of multimodal models. Transformer models, as the foundation of modern LLMs, offer a strong baseline with excellent scaling properties. However, the traditional transformer architecture requires substantial computations and poses significant obstacles for large-scale training and practical deployment. In this survey, we offer a systematic examination of innovative LLM architectures that address the inherent limitations of transformers and boost the efficiency. Starting from language modeling, this survey covers the background and technical details of linear and sparse sequence modeling methods, efficient full attention variants, sparse mixture-of-experts, hybrid model architectures incorporating the above techniques, and emerging diffusion LLMs. Additionally, we discuss applications of these techniques to other modalities and consider their wider implications for developing scalable, resource-aware foundation models. By grouping recent studies into the above category, this survey presents a blueprint of modern efficient LLM architectures, and we hope this could help motivate future research toward more efficient, versatile AI systems.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Linear Attention, Linear RNNs, and State Space Models (SSMs) show a convergent evolutionary trajectory, increasingly adopting similar mathematical formulations and optimization objectives to achieve linear complexity and constant memory.

Significant efficiency gains for standard softmax attention are achieved through hardware-aware optimizations (e.g., IO-aware attention like FlashAttention) and inference-time reductions (e.g., grouped attention, quantization) even without altering quadratic complexity.

Hybrid architectures, combining efficient linear models with attention mechanisms, and the emergence of non-autoregressive Diffusion LLMs represent promising directions for balancing performance and efficiency.
originalProblem: The Transformer architecture's quadratic computational and memory complexity with respect to sequence length creates significant barriers for training and deploying LLMs, especially for long-context and multimodal applications.
The rapid proliferation of efficiency-enhancing techniques for LLMs has led to a fragmented research landscape, making it difficult to navigate and understand the broader trends.
There is a critical need to reconcile the immense power of large foundation models with the practical demands of efficiency, scalability, and resource consciousness for sustainable AI development.
results: The survey maps a trend of 'linearization' of pre-trained Transformer models into efficient recurrent structures, often achieved via finetuning or distillation, to leverage existing model capabilities while gaining efficiency.

It demonstrates that Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) layers are a powerful paradigm for scaling model capacity without a proportional increase in computational cost, detailing advancements in routing and load balancing.

The research highlights the broad cross-modal transferability of these efficient architectural principles, with SSMs and MoE architectures being effectively adapted for diverse tasks in computer vision, audio processing, and complex multimodal settings.
solution: The authors conduct a comprehensive literature review, categorizing efficient LLM architectures into a systematic taxonomy with seven main categories: Linear Sequence Modeling, Sparse Sequence Modeling, Efficient Full Attention, Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE), Hybrid Architectures, Diffusion LLMs, and Applications to Other Modalities.
The survey delves into the technical details, mathematical formulations, key innovations, and representative models within each category, providing a structured overview.
It emphasizes the practical aspects of hardware-efficient implementations and critically analyzes the design principles, performance benefits, and limitations of each approach.
paper_summary.summary: This survey provides a systematic, unified taxonomy of efficient architectural designs and optimization strategies for Large Language Models (LLMs), addressing the computational and memory inefficiencies of traditional Transformers. It synthesizes current progress across diverse approaches, including linear and sparse models, optimized full attention, Mixture-of-Experts, and emerging Diffusion LLMs, also demonstrating their applicability to various modalities.

title: Speed Always Wins: A Survey on Efficient Architectures for Large Language Models

abstract: Language has long been conceived as an essential tool for human reasoning. The breakthrough of Large Language Models (LLMs) has sparked significant research interest in leveraging these models to tackle complex reasoning tasks. Researchers have moved beyond simple autoregressive token generation by introducing the concept of "thought" -- a sequence of tokens representing intermediate steps in the reasoning process. This innovative paradigm enables LLMs' to mimic complex human reasoning processes, such as tree search and reflective thinking. Recently, an emerging trend of learning to reason has applied reinforcement learning (RL) to train LLMs to master reasoning processes. This approach enables the automatic generation of high-quality reasoning trajectories through trial-and-error search algorithms, significantly expanding LLMs' reasoning capacity by providing substantially more training data. Furthermore, recent studies demonstrate that encouraging LLMs to "think" with more tokens during test-time inference can further significantly boost reasoning accuracy. Therefore, the train-time and test-time scaling combined to show a new research frontier -- a path toward Large Reasoning Model. The introduction of OpenAI's o1 series marks a significant milestone in this research direction. In this survey, we present a comprehensive review of recent progress in LLM reasoning. We begin by introducing the foundational background of LLMs and then explore the key technical components driving the development of large reasoning models, with a focus on automated data construction, learning-to-reason techniques, and test-time scaling. We also analyze popular open-source projects at building large reasoning models, and conclude with open challenges and future research directions.

keyInsights: Automated, LLM-driven data construction, particularly for generating step-by-step reasoning trajectories (Process Annotation), is crucial for scaling reasoning capabilities.
Reinforcement learning, especially through Process Reward Models (PRMs) providing dense, step-wise rewards, is pivotal for training LLMs to master complex reasoning processes.
Increasing computational effort during inference time through PRM-guided search methods can substantially enhance LLM reasoning accuracy, indicating a "test-time scaling law."
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) historically excelled at text generation but lacked robust, human-like complex reasoning capabilities.
A significant bottleneck for training LLMs on reasoning tasks was the high cost and difficulty of obtaining human-annotated, step-by-step reasoning data.
Bridging the gap between LLM language generation and sophisticated multi-step cognitive reasoning processes presented a key challenge.
results: The survey synthesizes advancements to define "Large Reasoning Models (LRMs)" as a new paradigm for advanced AI reasoning.
It maps the critical role of automated process data generation and reinforcement learning with Process Reward Models (PRMs) in enabling LLMs to learn complex reasoning.
It highlights the "test-time scaling law" where increased inference-time computation, guided by PRMs, consistently improves reasoning accuracy.
solution: Systematically reviews the field of "reinforced reasoning with LLMs," categorizing methods for data construction, learning, and test-time inference.
Focuses on the transition from human-annotated to LLM-driven automated data generation for reasoning trajectories.
Explores the application of reinforcement learning, particularly Process Reward Models (PRMs), and test-time computational scaling to enhance reasoning in LLMs.
paper_summary.summary: This Tsinghua University-led survey synthesizes advancements in reinforced reasoning with Large Language Models, identifying the emerging paradigm of "Large Reasoning Models (LRMs)." It outlines how these models integrate train-time improvements through reinforcement learning and automated data construction, particularly with Process Reward Models, alongside test-time computational scaling for enhanced reasoning.

title: Towards Large Reasoning Models: A Survey of Reinforced Reasoning with Large Language Models

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive reasoning capabilities, especially when guided by explicit chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning that verbalizes intermediate steps. While CoT improves both interpretability and accuracy, its dependence on natural language reasoning limits the model's expressive bandwidth. Latent reasoning tackles this bottleneck by performing multi-step inference entirely in the model's continuous hidden state, eliminating token-level supervision. To advance latent reasoning research, this survey provides a comprehensive overview of the emerging field of latent reasoning. We begin by examining the foundational role of neural network layers as the computational substrate for reasoning, highlighting how hierarchical representations support complex transformations. Next, we explore diverse latent reasoning methodologies, including activation-based recurrence, hidden state propagation, and fine-tuning strategies that compress or internalize explicit reasoning traces. Finally, we discuss advanced paradigms such as infinite-depth latent reasoning via masked diffusion models, which enable globally consistent and reversible reasoning processes. By unifying these perspectives, we aim to clarify the conceptual landscape of latent reasoning and chart future directions for research at the frontier of LLM cognition. An associated GitHub repository collecting the latest papers and repos is available at: this https URL.

keyInsights: Latent reasoning, operating within high-bandwidth continuous hidden states, offers a significant improvement over the low-bandwidth discrete tokens used in explicit reasoning.
Latent reasoning methodologies can be categorized into vertical recurrence (iterative refinement of activations) and horizontal recurrence (evolution of hidden states across sequences).
Mechanistic interpretability reveals that different LLM layers specialize in distinct aspects of latent reasoning, with intermediate layers performing core computational steps.
originalProblem: Large Language Models primarily rely on explicit Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning, which verbalizes intermediate steps in natural language.
This reliance on discrete tokens limits the model's expressive bandwidth compared to its continuous hidden states, which are significantly richer.
Forcing LLMs to verbalize every reasoning step introduces computational redundancy, as much of their complex processing occurs internally within hidden states.
results: Latent representations offer an approximately 2700 times higher bandwidth compared to explicit tokens, enabling richer internal expressivity.

The survey consolidates latent reasoning into two main paradigms: Vertical Recurrence (e.g., architectural loops like Universal Transformer, or training-induced recurrence like Coconut) and Horizontal Recurrence (e.g., Linear-State models like Mamba-2, or Gradient-State models like TTT).

Analysis of mechanistic interpretability shows that LLM layers exhibit specialized roles, with intermediate layers central to latent Chain-of-Thought, and highlights advanced paradigms for infinite-depth reasoning through spatial (text diffusion) and temporal (optimization-based) approaches.
solution: Defines and systematically surveys "Latent Reasoning" or "Latent Chain-of-Thought (Latent CoT)" as a paradigm for multi-step inference within an LLM's continuous hidden state.

Presents a comprehensive taxonomy that categorizes existing latent reasoning approaches based on how information is propagated, including vertical and horizontal recurrence, mechanistic interpretability, and infinite-depth reasoning.
Unifies disparate research efforts under a coherent conceptual framework to foster a clearer understanding of latent reasoning and chart future research directions.
paper_summary.summary: This comprehensive survey from a large multi-institutional collaboration examines "Latent Reasoning" in Large Language Models, an emerging paradigm that performs multi-step inference entirely within the model's high-bandwidth continuous hidden states to overcome the limitations of natural language-based explicit reasoning. It highlights the significant bandwidth advantage of latent representations (approximately 2700x higher) and provides a unified taxonomy of current methodologies.

title: A Survey on Latent Reasoning

abstract: Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) are rapidly emerging as a powerful and promising alternative to the dominant autoregressive (AR) paradigm. By generating tokens in parallel through an iterative denoising process, DLMs possess inherent advantages in reducing inference latency and capturing bidirectional context, thereby enabling fine-grained control over the generation process. While achieving a several-fold speed-up, recent advancements have allowed DLMs to show performance comparable to their autoregressive counterparts, making them a compelling choice for various natural language processing tasks. In this survey, we provide a holistic overview of the current DLM landscape. We trace its evolution and relationship with other paradigms, such as autoregressive and masked language models, and cover both foundational principles and state-of-the-art models. Our work offers an up-to-date, comprehensive taxonomy and an in-depth analysis of current techniques, from pre-training strategies to advanced post-training methods. Another contribution of this survey is a thorough review of DLM inference strategies and optimizations, including improvements in decoding parallelism, caching mechanisms, and generation quality. We also highlight the latest approaches to multimodal extensions of DLMs and delineate their applications across various practical scenarios. Furthermore, our discussion addresses the limitations and challenges of DLMs, including efficiency, long-sequence handling, and infrastructure requirements, while outlining future research directions to sustain progress in this rapidly evolving field. Project GitHub is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) represent a powerful alternative to autoregressive models, leveraging parallel generation, iterative refinement, and natural bidirectional context to address AR limitations.
DLMs have achieved performance comparable to, and in specific areas like reasoning and multimodal tasks, often superior to, state-of-the-art autoregressive counterparts.

The diffusion framework inherently supports unified multimodal AI, enabling seamless integration of text, image, and other modalities for both understanding and generation within single architectures.
originalProblem: Autoregressive (AR) models, long dominant in NLP, face inherent limitations like sequential inference bottlenecks and restricted control over the generation process.
The rapidly growing research on Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) was fragmented across various continuous, discrete, and hybrid approaches, lacking a comprehensive and systematic overview.
results: DLMs like LLaDA and Dream achieve competitive performance, often outperforming similarly sized AR models in benchmarks for reasoning, math (e.g., GSM8K), and code generation.
Multimodal DLMs such as MMaDA and LLaDA-V demonstrate strong capabilities, frequently surpassing AR-based multimodal models in integrated understanding and generation tasks.

Significant innovations in training (e.g., RL-based alignment like DoT, SEPO) and inference (e.g., parallel decoding, caching, step distillation) have enhanced DLM efficiency and quality, although challenges persist in infrastructure, long-sequence handling, and scalability.
solution: Conducting a systematic literature review to synthesize the diverse landscape of Diffusion Language Models, including their foundational theories, architectures, and evolution.
Developing a comprehensive taxonomy and timeline that categorizes DLM paradigms, training strategies (pre- and post-training), inference optimizations, and multimodal extensions.
Analyzing the performance, advantages, remaining challenges, and future research directions for DLMs across various language and multimodal tasks.
paper_summary.summary: This systematic survey from the VILA Lab at MBZUAI consolidates the rapidly expanding field of Diffusion Language Models (DLMs), outlining their foundational principles, diverse architectures, and training strategies. It demonstrates that DLMs offer competitive performance with autoregressive models, particularly excelling in parallel generation, bidirectional context modeling, and unified multimodal tasks.

title: A Survey on Diffusion Language Models

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong generalization across a wide range of tasks. Reasoning with LLMs is central to solving multi-step problems and complex decision-making. To support efficient reasoning, recent studies have shifted attention from explicit chain-of-thought prompting toward implicit reasoning, where reasoning occurs silently via latent structures without emitting intermediate textual steps. Implicit reasoning brings advantages such as lower generation cost, faster inference, and better alignment with internal computation. Although prior surveys have discussed latent representations in the context of reasoning, a dedicated and mechanism-level examination of how reasoning unfolds internally within LLMs remains absent. This survey fills that gap by introducing a taxonomy centered on execution paradigms, shifting the focus from representational forms to computational strategies. We organize existing methods into three execution paradigms based on \textbf{\textit{how and where internal computation unfolds}}: latent optimization, signal-guided control, and layer-recurrent execution. We also review structural, behavioral and representation-based evidence that supports the presence of implicit reasoning in LLMs. We further provide a structured overview of the evaluation metrics and benchmarks used in existing works to assess the effectiveness and reliability of implicit this http URL maintain a continuously updated project at: this https URL.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)

keyInsights: Implicit reasoning offers advantages over explicit methods, including lower generation costs, faster inference, and a potentially better alignment with the internal computational processes of LLMs.

LLMs exhibit verifiable patterns in their internal layers, observable behaviors, and detectable information in hidden states, all indicative of internal multi-step computations and implicit reasoning.

Current implicit reasoning methods face significant challenges related to interpretability, control, a performance gap compared to explicit CoT for complex tasks, and a lack of standardized evaluation protocols.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) often rely on explicit reasoning methods like Chain-of-Thought (CoT), which are inefficient due to generating lengthy textual intermediate steps, leading to high computational costs and inference latency.

The rapidly expanding field of implicit reasoning in LLMs, where multi-step computation occurs internally, lacks a unified conceptual framework and systematic categorization of its diverse approaches.
There is an underexplored need for a mechanism-level understanding of *how* internal reasoning unfolds within LLMs, beyond general discussions of latent representations.
results: The survey establishes a novel, three-part execution-centric taxonomy for implicit reasoning: Latent Optimization, Signal-Guided Control, and Layer-Recurrent Execution, providing a unified framework for the field.
It compiles and synthesizes comprehensive mechanistic and behavioral evidence, confirming the existence and operation of implicit reasoning within LLMs.

The work provides a structured overview of over 70 benchmark datasets and evaluation metrics, highlighting the inconsistencies in current practices and the need for standardized assessment of implicit reasoning methods.
solution: The survey introduces a novel, execution-centric taxonomy that categorizes implicit reasoning methods based on *how* and *where* internal computation takes place, rather than solely on representational forms.

It systematically details three primary execution paradigms: Latent Optimization (manipulating internal representations), Signal-Guided Control (using specialized non-textual tokens), and Layer-Recurrent Execution (iterative re-use of layers).
The authors synthesize structural, behavioral, and representation-based evidence to empirically demonstrate the presence and dynamics of implicit multi-step reasoning within LLMs.
paper_summary.summary: A comprehensive survey organizes research on implicit reasoning in Large Language Models (LLMs) by proposing a new execution-centric taxonomy and consolidating evidence for how multi-step reasoning unfolds internally without generating explicit text. The work highlights critical challenges and future directions to foster more efficient and robust LLM reasoning systems.

title: Implicit Reasoning in Large Language Models: A Comprehensive Survey

abstract: In this work, we provide a systematic survey of Discrete Diffusion Language Models (dLLMs) and Discrete Diffusion Multimodal Language Models (dMLLMs). Unlike autoregressive (AR) models, dLLMs and dMLLMs adopt a multi-token, parallel decoding paradigm using full attention and a denoising-based generation strategy. This paradigm naturally enables parallel generation, fine-grained output control, and dynamic perception. These capabilities are previously difficult to achieve with AR models. A growing number of industrial-scale proprietary d(M)LLMs, as well as a large number of open-source academic d(M)LLMs, have demonstrated performance comparable to their autoregressive counterparts, while achieving up to 10$\times$ acceleration in inference speed. These developments position discrete diffusion models as a promising alternative to intelligence based on the traditional autoregressive approach. In this work, we present a comprehensive overview of the research in the dLLM and dMLLM domains. We trace the historical development of dLLMs and dMLLMs, formalize the underlying mathematical frameworks, list commonly-used modeling methods, and categorize representative models. We further analyze key techniques for training, inference, quantization. We also discuss the trustworthy issues and summarize emerging applications across language, vision-language, and biological domains and etc.. We conclude by discussing future directions for research and deployment. Relative papers are collected in this https URL

keyInsights: Discrete diffusion models offer a viable alternative to autoregressive models, providing up to 10x faster inference speeds and improved control over generation.
The field is built on diverse mathematical foundations, including D3PMs, simplified masked diffusion, and continuous-time frameworks, each offering distinct approaches to the denoising process.

Discrete diffusion models have rapidly scaled and diversified across language and multimodal tasks, showcasing competitive performance and expanding applications in areas like reasoning, robotics, and drug discovery.
originalProblem: Autoregressive (AR) models suffer from slow sequential inference, preventing parallel generation of output tokens.
AR models exhibit limited fine-grained control over output attributes, often requiring complex prompt engineering.
The static, one-pass perception of input contexts in AR models hinders dynamic, task-aware comprehension.
results: The survey systematically consolidates fragmented research on discrete diffusion models, tracing their evolution and categorizing their mathematical frameworks, architectures, and techniques.

It details sophisticated training paradigms, such as diverse masking strategies, reweighting techniques, distillation, and reinforcement learning, alongside innovative inference methods like unmasking, remasking, and various guidance mechanisms.

The work identifies critical future research directions, including the development of robust infrastructure, further optimization of inference efficiency, and improved security and privacy safeguards tailored for d(M)LLMs.
solution: Discrete diffusion models reframe generation as an iterative denoising process over discrete token sequences, enabling parallel decoding for faster inference.
These models provide enhanced controllability over output properties by treating generation as a denoising or infilling task.
Bidirectional attention in discrete diffusion models allows for continuous revision of context perception throughout the generation process.
paper_summary.summary: This survey provides a systematic overview of Discrete Diffusion Language Models (dLLMs) and Multimodal Language Models (dMLLMs), detailing their mathematical foundations, modeling innovations, and diverse applications. It highlights how these models achieve up to 10 times faster inference and offer enhanced control compared to traditional autoregressive models, establishing them as a promising paradigm for future generative AI.

title: Discrete Diffusion in Large Language and Multimodal Models: A Survey

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown impressive performance on complex tasks through Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning. However, conventional CoT relies on explicitly verbalized intermediate steps, which constrains its broader applicability, particularly in abstract reasoning tasks beyond language. To address this, there has been growing research interest in \textit{latent CoT reasoning}, where the reasoning process is embedded within latent spaces. By decoupling reasoning from explicit language generation, latent CoT offers the promise of richer cognitive representations and facilitates more flexible, faster inference. This paper aims to present a comprehensive overview of this emerging paradigm and establish a systematic taxonomy. We analyze recent advances in methods, categorizing them from token-wise horizontal approaches to layer-wise vertical strategies. We then provide in-depth discussions of these methods, highlighting their design principles, applications, and remaining challenges. We hope that our survey provides a structured foundation for advancing this promising direction in LLM reasoning. The relevant papers will be regularly updated at this https URL.

keyInsights: Latent Chain-of-Thought reasoning offers advantages such as accelerated inference, richer and more compact cognitive representations, and the ability to explore multiple reasoning trajectories in parallel by decoupling reasoning from explicit linguistic tokens.
The proposed taxonomy effectively structures the diverse approaches in latent CoT, revealing fundamental differences in how latent thoughts are represented, optimized, and explored within LLMs.

Despite its potential, latent CoT faces significant challenges primarily due to the unobservable nature of its reasoning process, leading to difficulties in training stability, generalization to novel problems, and ensuring interpretability.
originalProblem: Existing explicit Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning in LLMs suffers from expressive redundancy, leading to inflated token usage, slower inference, and potential overfitting to stylistic artifacts.
Explicit CoT faces a semantic bottleneck as discrete language struggles to fully capture abstract, continuous, or multi-conceptual thought processes, limiting reasoning depth.

The rapidly expanding and fragmented field of latent CoT reasoning lacked a comprehensive, structured overview and taxonomy, making it difficult to understand diverse methodologies and identify research gaps.
results: The survey successfully established a unified taxonomy classifying latent CoT methods into token-wise horizontal and layer-wise vertical categories, clarifying the field's structural organization.
Latent CoT methods demonstrated practical utility across various domains, including textual reasoning, multimodal reasoning and generation, and information retrieval/recommendation systems.

Theoretical analyses and empirical findings suggest latent CoT can improve multi-step reasoning efficiency by solving structured problems with fewer steps and achieving reasoning depth through iterative computation.
solution: The paper proposes the first unified taxonomy for latent CoT, categorizing methods into 'token-wise horizontal' (sequential generation of latent thoughts) and 'layer-wise vertical' (iterative refinement of hidden states across layers) approaches.
It provides an in-depth discussion of representative latent CoT works, analyzing their design principles, training strategies, architectural paradigms, and efficiency trade-offs.
The survey identifies critical open problems in latent CoT, such as unsupervised training and interpretability, and outlines promising future research directions to guide the field.
paper_summary.summary: A comprehensive survey by Chen et al. (2025) introduces the first unified taxonomy for latent Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning, organizing a rapidly growing field into token-wise horizontal and layer-wise vertical approaches. It synthesizes current research, practical applications, and outlines critical challenges for future advancements in LLM efficiency and cognitive capabilities.

title: Reasoning Beyond Language: A Comprehensive Survey on Latent Chain-of-Thought Reasoning

abstract: Recently, tool learning with large language models (LLMs) has emerged as a promising paradigm for augmenting the capabilities of LLMs to tackle highly complex problems. Despite growing attention and rapid advancements in this field, the existing literature remains fragmented and lacks systematic organization, posing barriers to entry for newcomers. This gap motivates us to conduct a comprehensive survey of existing works on tool learning with LLMs. In this survey, we focus on reviewing existing literature from the two primary aspects (1) why tool learning is beneficial and (2) how tool learning is implemented, enabling a comprehensive understanding of tool learning with LLMs. We first explore the "why" by reviewing both the benefits of tool integration and the inherent benefits of the tool learning paradigm from six specific aspects. In terms of "how", we systematically review the literature according to a taxonomy of four key stages in the tool learning workflow: task planning, tool selection, tool calling, and response generation. Additionally, we provide a detailed summary of existing benchmarks and evaluation methods, categorizing them according to their relevance to different stages. Finally, we discuss current challenges and outline potential future directions, aiming to inspire both researchers and industrial developers to further explore this emerging and promising area. We also maintain a GitHub repository to continually keep track of the relevant papers and resources in this rising area at this https URL.

keyInsights: Tool learning fundamentally enhances Large Language Models (LLMs) by overcoming inherent limitations such as factual inaccuracies, computational deficiencies, and lack of real-world interaction, while also improving interpretability and robustness.

The process of integrating tools with LLMs can be systematically broken down into four distinct stages: task planning, tool selection, tool calling, and response generation, each involving specific methodologies.

Despite rapid advancements, the field faces challenges including high latency, the need for more rigorous evaluation and comprehensive toolsets, and developing unified, safe, and multi-modal tool learning frameworks.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) inherently struggle with complex computations, are prone to generating factually incorrect or outdated information (hallucinations), and lack the ability to interact with the real world or execute external actions.
The rapidly growing body of research on tool learning with LLMs was fragmented and lacked a systematic organization, posing barriers for newcomers and hindering comprehensive understanding.
Existing surveys typically addressed tools or tool learning only peripherally, failing to provide a focused and detailed analysis of this specific and emerging paradigm.
results: The survey thoroughly outlined the multi-faceted benefits of tool integration for LLMs, including knowledge acquisition, expertise enhancement, automation, interaction enhancement, improved interpretability, and increased robustness and adaptability.

A structured four-stage workflow for tool learning was presented: Task Planning, Tool Selection, Tool Calling, and Response Generation, with detailed taxonomies of both tuning-free and tuning-based methods for each stage.

A comprehensive summary of 33 benchmarks, key open-source toolkits (e.g., LangChain, Auto-GPT), and specific evaluation metrics for each stage of tool learning was compiled, and seven critical future research directions were identified.
solution: The authors conducted a systematic and comprehensive survey specifically focused on tool learning with Large Language Models, addressing the existing fragmentation in literature.

The survey categorized and synthesized existing research around two central questions: 'why tool learning is beneficial' and 'how tool learning is implemented', covering benefits, methodologies, benchmarks, and challenges.

The 'how' aspect was detailed through a four-stage workflow for tool learning (Task Planning, Tool Selection, Tool Calling, Response Generation), with a review of existing methods for each stage, distinguishing between tuning-free and tuning-based approaches.
paper_summary.summary: A systematic and comprehensive review of tool learning with Large Language Models is presented, organizing fragmented literature by detailing the 'why' and 'how' of LLM-tool integration, surveying existing methods, benchmarks, and identifying open challenges and future research directions.

title: Tool Learning with Large Language Models: A Survey

abstract:
Recent efforts have augmented large language models (LLMs) with external
resources (e.g., the Internet) or internal control flows (e.g., prompt
chaining) for tasks requiring grounding or reasoning, leading to a new class of
language agents. While these agents have achieved substantial empirical
success, we lack a systematic framework to organize existing agents and plan
future developments. In this paper, we draw on the rich history of cognitive
science and symbolic artificial intelligence to propose Cognitive Architectures
for Language Agents (CoALA). CoALA describes a language agent with modular
memory components, a structured action space to interact with internal memory
and external environments, and a generalized decision-making process to choose
actions. We use CoALA to retrospectively survey and organize a large body of
recent work, and prospectively identify actionable directions towards more
capable agents. Taken together, CoALA contextualizes today's language agents
within the broader history of AI and outlines a path towards language-based
general intelligence.

keyInsights: Modern large language models can be effectively understood as "probabilistic production systems," with prompt engineering acting as their control flow.
Historical cognitive architectures like Soar provide a valuable blueprint for structuring and understanding the internal processes (memory, decision-making, learning) of contemporary language agents.

Language agents require modular memory components (working, episodic, semantic, procedural) and a structured action space (external grounding, internal reasoning, retrieval, and learning) to achieve complex behaviors.
originalProblem: The rapidly growing field of language agents lacked a unifying theoretical framework to systematically understand, compare, and develop these AI systems.
Inconsistent terminology across different research efforts made it challenging to compare and build upon existing language agent designs.
There was a need for a prescriptive design guide, rooted in historical AI and cognitive science insights, to direct future language agent development beyond empirical surveys.
results: CoALA successfully categorizes a large body of existing language agents (e.g., SayCan, ReAct, Voyager), illustrating their architectural commonalities and distinguishing features.

The framework provides precise, modular definitions for agent memory types (working, episodic, semantic, procedural), action types (external, internal), and a generalized decision-making loop (planning and execution).

Systematic application of CoALA identifies significant underexplored areas in current language agent research, such as advanced learning mechanisms, adaptive retrieval, and more sophisticated decision-making processes.
solution: The paper proposes CoALA, a conceptual framework positioning large language models (LLMs) as probabilistic production systems within a larger cognitive architecture.
CoALA defines three core dimensions for language agents: modular memory components, a structured action space, and a generalized decision-making process.
The framework is developed by drawing analogies between modern LLMs and historical production systems, and between contemporary language agents and classical cognitive architectures like Soar.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Princeton University introduce CoALA, a conceptual framework that leverages historical cognitive architectures and production systems to organize and guide the design of language agents. It demonstrates CoALA's utility by systematically categorizing existing agents and identifying directions for developing more capable AI systems.

title: Cognitive Architectures for Language Agents

abstract: As text generation has become a core capability of modern Large Language Models (LLMs), it underpins a wide range of downstream applications. However, most existing LLMs rely on autoregressive (AR) generation, producing one token at a time based on previously generated context-resulting in limited generation speed due to the inherently sequential nature of the process. To address this challenge, an increasing number of researchers have begun exploring parallel text generation-a broad class of techniques aimed at breaking the token-by-token generation bottleneck and improving inference efficiency. Despite growing interest, there remains a lack of comprehensive analysis on what specific techniques constitute parallel text generation and how they improve inference performance. To bridge this gap, we present a systematic survey of parallel text generation methods. We categorize existing approaches into AR-based and Non-AR-based paradigms, and provide a detailed examination of the core techniques within each category. Following this taxonomy, we assess their theoretical trade-offs in terms of speed, quality, and efficiency, and examine their potential for combination and comparison with alternative acceleration strategies. Finally, based on our findings, we highlight recent advancements, identify open challenges, and outline promising directions for future research in parallel text generation. We have also created a GitHub repository for indexing relevant papers and open resources available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)

keyInsights: The field of parallel text generation can be systematically organized by distinguishing methods that preserve AR semantic dependency during training from those that break it.

Different parallel generation paradigms exhibit distinct trade-offs, for example, Draft-and-Verifying offers high quality with moderate speedup (1.8x-2.4x), while Masked Generation can achieve significantly higher speedups (up to 32x) with quality dependent on refinement steps.

Many parallel generation techniques are composable (e.g., MTP with Draft-and-Verify) and compatible with other acceleration strategies (like model compression), allowing for synergistic performance gains.
originalProblem: Most Large Language Models (LLMs) rely on autoregressive (AR) generation, producing text token-by-token, which inherently limits generation speed.
This sequential generation leads to high inference latency and suboptimal hardware utilization, especially for long outputs or real-time applications.
A lack of a systematic and unified analysis exists for the diverse landscape of parallel text generation methods designed to overcome this AR bottleneck.
results: Six primary paradigms for parallel text generation were identified and detailed: Draft-and-Verifying, Decomposition-and-Fill, Multiple Token Prediction, One-Shot Generation, Masked Generation, and Edit-Based Refinement.

Theoretical speedups varied significantly across paradigms: One-Shot Generation achieved the highest potential (10x-16.5x), followed by Masked Generation (up to 32x), while AR-based methods typically provided moderate gains (1.8x-5.7x).

The survey outlined specific challenges and promising future directions, including optimizing implementation, achieving better quality-speed trade-offs, improving integration with existing LLM ecosystems, and developing unified tooling.
solution: The authors developed a systematic survey and hierarchical taxonomy, categorizing parallel text generation methods into AR-based (Draft-and-Verifying, Decomposition-and-Fill, Multiple Token Prediction) and Non-AR-based (One-Shot, Masked, Edit-Based Refinement) paradigms.
The survey provides detailed examinations of the core mechanisms, variations, and theoretical underpinnings for each category.

It conducts a comparative analysis of these paradigms across key dimensions: decoding speed, output quality, and resource efficiency, also exploring their composability and compatibility with other acceleration techniques.
paper_summary.summary: A comprehensive survey categorizes parallel text generation techniques for Large Language Models (LLMs), outlining their mechanisms, comparative trade-offs in decoding speed, output quality, and resource usage. The analysis unifies diverse approaches to accelerate LLM inference, from parallel decoding to diffusion models.

title: A Survey on Parallel Text Generation: From Parallel Decoding to Diffusion Language Models

abstract:
Embodied multimodal large models (EMLMs) have gained significant attention in
recent years due to their potential to bridge the gap between perception,
cognition, and action in complex, real-world environments. This comprehensive
review explores the development of such models, including Large Language Models
(LLMs), Large Vision Models (LVMs), and other models, while also examining
other emerging architectures. We discuss the evolution of EMLMs, with a focus
on embodied perception, navigation, interaction, and simulation. Furthermore,
the review provides a detailed analysis of the datasets used for training and
evaluating these models, highlighting the importance of diverse, high-quality
data for effective learning. The paper also identifies key challenges faced by
EMLMs, including issues of scalability, generalization, and real-time
decision-making. Finally, we outline future directions, emphasizing the
integration of multimodal sensing, reasoning, and action to advance the
development of increasingly autonomous systems. By providing an in-depth
analysis of state-of-the-art methods and identifying critical gaps, this paper
aims to inspire future advancements in EMLMs and their applications across
diverse domains.

organization_info.name: Guangdong Laboratory of Artificial Intelligence and Digital Economy

keyInsights: EMLMs are increasingly integrating LLMs and LVLMs to enhance capabilities in embodied perception, navigation, and interaction.
Simulation environments and diverse, high-quality multimodal datasets are crucial for training and evaluating EMLMs.
Key challenges include cross-modal alignment, computational efficiency, generalization, temporal information handling, and dataset diversity.
originalProblem: Existing literature reviews often focus narrowly on LLMs or LVMs, overlooking their integration with embodied agents.
Previous reviews either covered embodied intelligence too broadly or too narrowly, lacking a 'full-stack' analysis.
A systematic and timely overview of Embodied Multimodal Large Models (EMLMs) was needed to consolidate rapid advancements.
results: The review systematically categorizes EMLM development based on embodied perception, navigation, and interaction, detailing various models and approaches.
It provides a comprehensive overview of foundational models (LLMs, LVMs, LVLMs) and their role in EMLMs.
It summarizes critical benchmark datasets and simulation environments essential for EMLM research and development.
solution: The paper conducts the first systematic review of Embodied Multimodal Large Models (EMLMs).
It analyzes approximately 300 research papers to provide a 'full-stack' understanding of EMLMs, covering foundational models, embodied components, simulation, and datasets.
It identifies current main findings, key challenges, and promising future research directions for the field.
paper_summary.summary: This paper presents a comprehensive review of Embodied Multimodal Large Models (EMLMs), analyzing their development, integration of large language and vision models, and the role of datasets and simulation environments. It highlights advancements in embodied perception, navigation, and interaction, while outlining persistent challenges and future research directions.

title: Exploring Embodied Multimodal Large Models: Development, Datasets, and  Future Directions

abstract: Transformer-based architectures have become the prevailing backbone of large language models. However, the quadratic time and memory complexity of self-attention remains a fundamental obstacle to efficient long-context modeling. To address this limitation, recent research has introduced two principal categories of efficient attention mechanisms. Linear attention methods achieve linear complexity through kernel approximations, recurrent formulations, or fastweight dynamics, thereby enabling scalable inference with reduced computational overhead. Sparse attention techniques, in contrast, limit attention computation to selected subsets of tokens based on fixed patterns, block-wise routing, or clustering strategies, enhancing efficiency while preserving contextual coverage. This survey provides a systematic and comprehensive overview of these developments, integrating both algorithmic innovations and hardware-level considerations. In addition, we analyze the incorporation of efficient attention into largescale pre-trained language models, including both architectures built entirely on efficient attention and hybrid designs that combine local and global components. By aligning theoretical foundations with practical deployment strategies, this work aims to serve as a foundational reference for advancing the design of scalable and efficient language models.

keyInsights: Efficient attention mechanisms are broadly divided into Linear Attention, which re-formulates or approximates softmax-based attention into operations with linear complexity, and Sparse Attention, which restricts attention computation to a selected subset of tokens.

Practical efficiency in LLMs requires careful integration of algorithmic innovations with hardware-friendly design principles, considering factors such as memory access patterns, GPU utilization, and kernel-level optimizations.

Modern LLM architectures increasingly adopt either uniformly efficient attention designs or hybrid approaches that strategically combine different attention types (e.g., local, global, sparse, linear) to balance computational efficiency with comprehensive context modeling.
originalProblem: The standard self-attention mechanism in Transformer-based Large Language Models (LLMs) suffers from quadratic time and memory complexity concerning input sequence length.
This quadratic complexity creates a significant bottleneck, limiting LLMs' ability to efficiently process very long contexts.
The computational limitations hinder the practical scalability of LLMs for tasks requiring extensive contextual understanding, such as analyzing long documents or complex reasoning.
results: A systematic taxonomy of efficient attention is presented, detailing kernel approximations, recurrent formulations, and in-context learning interpretations for Linear Attention, alongside fixed-pattern, block-sparse, and clustering strategies for Sparse Attention.

Hardware-aligned design principles are identified, highlighting parallel, recurrent, and chunkwise representations for linear attention and emphasizing block-level granularity and GPU primitive alignment (like FlashAttention) for sparse attention.

The survey showcases how efficient attention is integrated into various pre-trained LLMs, including models built entirely on efficient architectures (e.g., RWKV, Mamba-based models) and prevalent hybrid designs that interleave different attention types to achieve scalability and performance.
solution: The survey systematically categorizes efficient attention mechanisms into two principal types: Linear Attention and Sparse Attention.

It provides detailed breakdowns of algorithmic innovations within each category, including sub-classifications like kernelized linear attention, recurrent formulations with forgetting mechanisms, fixed-pattern sparse attention, and routing-based block-sparse attention.

The work integrates practical hardware-level considerations for implementation and analyzes how these efficient mechanisms are deployed in both uniformly efficient and hybrid pre-trained LLM architectures.
paper_summary.summary: Tsinghua University researchers provide a comprehensive survey of efficient attention mechanisms for Large Language Models, classifying approaches into linear and sparse attention while integrating algorithmic designs, hardware considerations, and their application in pre-trained models. The work offers a structured understanding of methods developed to address the quadratic computational costs of standard self-attention.

title: Efficient Attention Mechanisms for Large Language Models: A Survey

abstract: The rapid emergence of Large Language Models (LLMs) has precipitated a profound paradigm shift in Artificial Intelligence, delivering monumental engineering successes that increasingly impact modern society. However, a critical paradox persists within the current field: despite the empirical efficacy, our theoretical understanding of LLMs remains disproportionately nascent, forcing these systems to be treated largely as ``black boxes''. To address this theoretical fragmentation, this survey proposes a unified lifecycle-based taxonomy that organizes the research landscape into six distinct stages: Data Preparation, Model Preparation, Training, Alignment, Inference, and Evaluation. Within this framework, we provide a systematic review of the foundational theories and internal mechanisms driving LLM performance. Specifically, we analyze core theoretical issues such as the mathematical justification for data mixtures, the representational limits of various architectures, and the optimization dynamics of alignment algorithms. Moving beyond current best practices, we identify critical frontier challenges, including the theoretical limits of synthetic data self-improvement, the mathematical bounds of safety guarantees, and the mechanistic origins of emergent intelligence. By connecting empirical observations with rigorous scientific inquiry, this work provides a structured roadmap for transitioning LLM development from engineering heuristics toward a principled scientific discipline.
title: Beyond the Black Box: A Survey on the Theory and Mechanism of Large Language Models

abstract: The performance of modern AI systems is fundamentally constrained by the quality of their underlying kernels, which translate high-level algorithmic semantics into low-level hardware operations. Achieving near-optimal kernels requires expert-level understanding of hardware architectures and programming models, making kernel engineering a critical but notoriously time-consuming and non-scalable process. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) and LLM-based agents have opened new possibilities for automating kernel generation and optimization. LLMs are well-suited to compress expert-level kernel knowledge that is difficult to formalize, while agentic systems further enable scalable optimization by casting kernel development as an iterative, feedback-driven loop. Rapid progress has been made in this area. However, the field remains fragmented, lacking a systematic perspective for LLM-driven kernel generation. This survey addresses this gap by providing a structured overview of existing approaches, spanning LLM-based approaches and agentic optimization workflows, and systematically compiling the datasets and benchmarks that underpin learning and evaluation in this domain. Moreover, key open challenges and future research directions are further outlined, aiming to establish a comprehensive reference for the next generation of automated kernel optimization. To keep track of this field, we maintain an open-source GitHub repository at this https URL.

keyInsights: LLMs can be effectively specialized for kernel generation through supervised fine-tuning on high-quality paired datasets and enhanced through reinforcement learning with sophisticated reward mechanisms, enabling the synthesis of optimized low-level code.

LLM-based agents significantly advance kernel optimization by employing iterative refinement, leveraging external knowledge bases, integrating hardware-specific profiling, and coordinating complex tasks through multi-agent collaboration, moving beyond static code generation.

The rapid development of domain-specific data infrastructure, including structured datasets, code repositories, and authoritative knowledge bases, is critical for training and improving the accuracy and performance of LLM-driven kernel generation systems.
originalProblem: Developing and optimizing hardware kernels, low-level programs fundamental to AI operations, is a time-consuming and specialized process requiring deep understanding of intricate hardware architectures.

The 'kernel engineering bottleneck' limits the scalability and portability of AI systems, as manual optimization lacks generalization across diverse hardware and workloads, hindering the efficient deployment of rapidly scaling large language models.
Custom kernel implementations are often hardware-specific, requiring extensive human expertise and preventing efficient reuse across different computing platforms.
results: Diverse LLM-based approaches, including SFT methods like ConCuR's KernelCoder and RL-driven techniques such as Kevin and QiMeng-Kernel, have demonstrated success in specializing LLMs for kernel generation, with some RL methods even surpassing hand-optimized libraries.

LLM-based agents, exemplified by Caesar, PEAK, and FM Agent, show the ability to iteratively refine and optimize kernels, integrating external memory and hardware-aware feedback to achieve better performance.

Kernel benchmarking practices are evolving towards more comprehensive and realistic evaluations, incorporating diverse metrics beyond basic correctness and expanding coverage to include various hardware platforms (NVIDIA, HUAWEI, Google, AMD) and real-world LLM workloads.
solution: Leveraging large language models (LLMs) for kernel code synthesis, employing techniques such as supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on curated datasets and reinforcement learning (RL) with iterative feedback to generate optimized low-level code.

Developing LLM-based agents that autonomously orchestrate kernel optimization through iterative refinement, integration of external memory for knowledge access, incorporation of hardware profiling feedback, and coordination of multi-agent workflows.
Systematically compiling essential datasets, code-centric corpora, and knowledge bases to train, evaluate, and support LLM-driven and agentic kernel generation systems.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from BAAI, Peking University, and other institutions provide a comprehensive survey on LLM-driven kernel generation, systematically categorizing existing methodologies, compiling essential datasets and benchmarks, and identifying critical challenges and future research directions in this rapidly evolving field. The survey highlights the emergence of diverse LLM-based and agentic approaches, along with the growing data infrastructure and evolving benchmarking practices.

title: Towards Automated Kernel Generation in the Era of LLMs

abstract: The rapid advancement of large language models (LLMs) has intensified the need for effective mechanisms to transform continuous multimodal data into discrete representations suitable for language-based processing. Discrete tokenization, with vector quantization (VQ) as a central approach, offers both computational efficiency and compatibility with LLM architectures. Despite its growing importance, there is a lack of a comprehensive survey that systematically examines VQ techniques in the context of LLM-based systems. This work fills this gap by presenting the first structured taxonomy and analysis of discrete tokenization methods designed for LLMs. We categorize 8 representative VQ variants that span classical and modern paradigms and analyze their algorithmic principles, training dynamics, and integration challenges with LLM pipelines. Beyond algorithm-level investigation, we discuss existing research in terms of classical applications without LLMs, LLM-based single-modality systems, and LLM-based multimodal systems, highlighting how quantization strategies influence alignment, reasoning, and generation performance. In addition, we identify key challenges including codebook collapse, unstable gradient estimation, and modality-specific encoding constraints. Finally, we discuss emerging research directions such as dynamic and task-adaptive quantization, unified tokenization frameworks, and biologically inspired codebook learning. This survey bridges the gap between traditional vector quantization and modern LLM applications, serving as a foundational reference for the development of efficient and generalizable multimodal systems. A continuously updated version is available at: this https URL.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)

keyInsights: Vector Quantization (VQ) and its variants serve as a crucial bridge, transforming continuous multimodal data into discrete tokens that are computationally efficient and compatible with LLM architectures.

Addressing challenges like codebook collapse through strategies such as code reset or specific VQ designs (FSQ, LFQ) and managing non-differentiable gradient propagation (e.g., STE, Gumbel-Softmax) are central to robust tokenization.

The design principles from earlier VQ applications in areas like image retrieval and audio compression remain relevant and inform the development of advanced modality-specific tokenization strategies for current LLM integrations.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) fundamentally process discrete, token-based inputs, presenting a challenge when integrating with continuous, high-dimensional multimodal data.
There is a need for efficient and compatible mechanisms to convert continuous multimodal signals (e.g., images, audio) into discrete tokens suitable for LLM architectures.
A systematic and comprehensive survey on Vector Quantization (VQ) techniques, specifically tailored for LLM-based multimodal systems, was missing in existing literature.
results: A detailed taxonomy of eight VQ variants (e.g., VQ, RVQ, PQ, FSQ, GART) was established, outlining their mechanisms for discretizing continuous data and addressing issues like codebook collapse.
Critical challenges in discrete tokenization for LLMs, including information loss, gradient propagation, and semantic alignment, were identified and discussed with proposed mitigation strategies.

The paper demonstrated the evolutionary impact of discrete tokenization, from its foundational role in pre-LLM applications to its current position as a core component enabling LLMs to effectively process and generate across diverse single and multiple modalities.
solution: The authors established a comprehensive taxonomy that categorizes and organizes eight prominent Vector Quantization (VQ) variants, detailing their codebook learning paradigms and integration with LLM pipelines.

An in-depth algorithmic investigation was conducted for each VQ variant, covering principles, codebook initialization/updating, gradient propagation through discrete bottlenecks, and common issues like codebook collapse.

The survey provided an application-level review of discrete tokenization across classical settings, single-modality LLM systems, and complex multimodal LLM systems (e.g., text-image, text-audio), highlighting their evolution and impact.
paper_summary.summary: This work presents a comprehensive survey and structured taxonomy of discrete tokenization techniques, particularly Vector Quantization (VQ), for Multimodal Large Language Models (LLMs). It analyzes eight VQ variants, their algorithmic principles, and application across various modalities, bridging the knowledge gap between traditional VQ and its role in modern LLM-based systems.

title: Discrete Tokenization for Multimodal LLMs: A Comprehensive Survey

abstract:
Language models serve as a cornerstone in natural language processing (NLP),
utilizing mathematical methods to generalize language laws and knowledge for
prediction and generation. Over extensive research spanning decades, language
modeling has progressed from initial statistical language models (SLMs) to the
contemporary landscape of large language models (LLMs). Notably, the swift
evolution of LLMs has reached the ability to process, understand, and generate
human-level text. Nevertheless, despite the significant advantages that LLMs
offer in improving both work and personal lives, the limited understanding
among general practitioners about the background and principles of these models
hampers their full potential. Notably, most LLM reviews focus on specific
aspects and utilize specialized language, posing a challenge for practitioners
lacking relevant background knowledge. In light of this, this survey aims to
present a comprehensible overview of LLMs to assist a broader audience. It
strives to facilitate a comprehensive understanding by exploring the historical
background of language models and tracing their evolution over time. The survey
further investigates the factors influencing the development of LLMs,
emphasizing key contributions. Additionally, it concentrates on elucidating the
underlying principles of LLMs, equipping audiences with essential theoretical
knowledge. The survey also highlights the limitations of existing work and
points out promising future directions.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: The rapid advancement of LLMs is largely attributable to the confluence of vast data diversity, exponential computational growth, and algorithmic innovations like the Transformer architecture.
LLMs exhibit emergent abilities and adaptability, often achieved through alignment with human values rather than extensive domain-specific fine-tuning.

Despite their broad capabilities, LLMs face substantial challenges related to privacy risks, fairness issues stemming from inherited biases, safety concerns like 'hallucination,' and complex intellectual property considerations.
originalProblem: Existing Large Language Model (LLM) reviews often use specialized language and focus on specific aspects, challenging general practitioners without deep technical backgrounds.
There is a limited understanding among general practitioners regarding the historical context, underlying principles, and operational mechanisms of LLMs.
A lack of accessible educational resources hinders the effective understanding, utilization, and responsible deployment of LLMs across various professional domains.
results: The survey delineates the evolution of language models through four distinct stages: Statistical, Neural, Pre-trained, and Large Language Models.
It identifies and explains the fundamental principles of LLMs, illustrating them using the GPT-3 architecture, including input processing, masked multi-head self-attention, and decoding mechanisms.
A taxonomy classifies LLMs into encoder-only, encoder-decoder, and decoder-only architectures, accompanied by a comprehensive comparison table of state-of-the-art models and their attributes.
Transformative applications of LLMs are highlighted across diverse industries, including software engineering, drug discovery, finance, medicine, legal, and education.
solution: The authors conducted a meticulous review of prominent academic databases (IEEE Xplore, ACM Digital Library, Scopus, Google Scholar) using a structured keyword strategy.
They synthesized relevant literature from major NLP and AI conferences and journals published within the past decade, prioritizing impact, relevance, and methodological rigor.

The survey provides a comprehensive yet comprehensible overview of LLM evolution, core principles (e.g., Transformer architecture), influencing factors, diverse applications, and critical ethical drawbacks for a broad audience.
paper_summary.summary: This survey paper from Florida International University and Shenzhen Institutes of Advanced Technology offers an accessible overview of Large Language Models, detailing their historical progression, foundational principles, and diverse applications. It critically examines the factors driving their rapid development and addresses inherent drawbacks such as privacy, fairness, and safety, aiming to equip a broad audience with fundamental knowledge.

title: History, Development, and Principles of Large Language Models-An  Introductory Survey

abstract: This survey explores the burgeoning field of role-playing with language models, focusing on their development from early persona-based models to advanced character-driven simulations facilitated by Large Language Models (LLMs). Initially confined to simple persona consistency due to limited model capabilities, role-playing tasks have now expanded to embrace complex character portrayals involving character consistency, behavioral alignment, and overall attractiveness. We provide a comprehensive taxonomy of the critical components in designing these systems, including data, models and alignment, agent architecture and evaluation. This survey not only outlines the current methodologies and challenges, such as managing dynamic personal profiles and achieving high-level persona consistency but also suggests avenues for future research in improving the depth and realism of role-playing applications. The goal is to guide future research by offering a structured overview of current methodologies and identifying potential areas for improvement. Related resources and papers are available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)

keyInsights: The field has evolved from simple persona-based role-playing (P-RP) to sophisticated character-based role-playing (C-RP), necessitating granular character information and advanced LLM capabilities.
Achieving precise character alignment is the central and persistent challenge, employing various parameter-tuning and parameter-frozen methods with distinct trade-offs.

Evaluation of role-playing models is inherently complex, requiring multi-dimensional assessment beyond basic conversation ability, and current evaluation practices suffer from significant limitations in metrics, bias, and scalability.
originalProblem: The rapidly expanding field of role-playing with language models lacked a unified framework and systematic understanding, leading to fragmented research.
Human desires for AI interactions extended beyond generic helpfulness to psychological and entertainment needs, requiring complex character portrayals.
Persistent challenges, such as managing dynamic personal profiles, ensuring high-level persona consistency, and mitigating hallucination, were not clearly articulated across diverse methodologies.
results: Introduced a four-part taxonomy (Data, Models & Alignment, Agent Architecture, Evaluation) that provides a structured framework for understanding and optimizing role-playing language models.
Identified and detailed 10 major challenges, including the need for better reference metrics, addressing LLM-based evaluation sensitivities, combating character hallucination, and ensuring safety.
Provided a critical analysis of current evaluation approaches (reference-based, human-based, LLM-based), highlighting their deficiencies and calling for more robust and verifiable methodologies.
solution: Developed a systematic taxonomy to organize the field into four key components: Data, Models & Alignment, Agent Architecture, and Evaluation.
Conducted a comprehensive literature review, tracing the evolution from persona-based to character-based role-playing with language models.
Analyzed current methodologies for data collection, model training, alignment techniques, and agent architectures (memory, planning, action modules), alongside evaluation strategies.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from HKUST, Tencent, and Singapore Management University present a comprehensive survey and taxonomy for language model-based role-playing, categorizing advancements in data, models, agent architecture, and evaluation.
